title: The Faults of Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: The Faults of Evangelion             Quote Post by BeoX2 » Wed Jul 14, 2010 10:54 pm I know that we all love Eva here, and don't get me wrong I love it too, but what do you think the faults were with it, or things that Anno should have done. I know that the pros of the show greatly outweigh the cons, but nothing is perfect. Personally, I think that Shinji's character is too stagnant. In fact, they all kind of are. You get to know the characters so well in their current state (AKA, shit) and you never really get to see them evolve. It's not really character development, it's like character analyzing. I like this, but I feel as though that Anno could have made them evolve more. My only other fault with the series (besides the technical animation stuff. Seriously, near the end, it sucked) was the final two episodes. NOW DON'T GET ME WRONG HERE! I love these episodes. I consider them to be one of the most fantastic conclusions to any series in any form of media. I wish I could one day write a story like that. The problem is that it just ended, right there. I thought that there should have been like maybe 4 more episodes, to chronicle the physical side of it and how Shinji uses his new knowledge to better himself and those around him. People may say that 3I had already started, but the final scene in the conventional Evangelion was him and Misato on the beach, not the Anti-AT field spreading. There could have been more, and less haters with it.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Jul 14, 2010 11:06 pm See HERE and HERE and HERE. As for your complaints, I guess I understand the complaint that the characters "don't evolve" but I think it's wrongheaded in the respect that NGE is a character study instead of being a traditional drama with a character arc (like, say, Gone With the Wind). IOW, the characters are a certain way, and the series is going to go progressively deeper in examining why they are that way. Plus, I said of EoE that it reveals that the entire point of the series is to reach the point where change is possible, rather than to show the change itself. NGE is about finding the way to make that first step towards betterment. As for the ending, some just have a problem with irresolution. Knowing what happened to the characters after NGE really doesn't deepen the work in any substantial way, while, paradoxically, not knowing kinda does.

[]:             Quote Post by BeoX2 » Wed Jul 14, 2010 11:21 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: See HERE and HERE and HERE. As for your complaints, I guess I understand the complaint that the characters "don't evolve" but I think it's wrongheaded in the respect that NGE is a character study instead of being a traditional drama with a character arc (like, say, Gone With the Wind). IOW, the characters are a certain way, and the series is going to go progressively deeper in examining why they are that way. Plus, I said of EoE that it reveals that the entire point of the series is to reach the point where change is possible, rather than to show the change itself. NGE is about finding the way to make that first step towards betterment. As for the ending, some just have a problem with irresolution. Knowing what happened to the characters after NGE really doesn't deepen the work in any substantial way, while, paradoxically, not knowing kinda does.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Jul 14, 2010 11:29 pm EoE is literally the end of the series. Any discussion of NGE includes EoE as part of its overall canon.

[]:             Quote Post by BeoX2 » Wed Jul 14, 2010 11:32 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: EoE is literally the end of the series. Any discussion of NGE includes EoE as part of its overall canon.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Jul 15, 2010 12:17 am BeoX2 wrote: And it is separate in a sense, because it was released after Anno had time to address the negative aspects.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Thu Jul 15, 2010 12:29 am The writing seems to intentionally work its way around some things that would have a decent chance of coming up in conversation. Like "So Rei, what are your parents like?" There also seems to be a disconnect between characters being seemingly able to interact on fairly amiable and open terms whenever they're in the same frame, and the show actually giving them the chance to do that for more than 30 seconds at a time before the scene changes or something else happens to ensure that the status quo is preserved. As such, while I love NGE cinematically, I love its characters, and I think it has a powerful story, I don't think it holds up as a particularly believable narrative under close scrutiny. Unless I'm supposed to operate under the impression that the characters are put into small, separate shoeboxes whenever the camera leaves the room, I don't think the angst and supposed inability of the characters to connect holds up. Because for the most part they sure as heck seem to talk to each other just fine when they're actually onscreen at the same time. I've brought this up before and have heard varying attempts at explanations for it.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Jul 15, 2010 12:36 am esselfortium wrote: I've brought this up before and have heard varying attempts at explanations for it.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Thu Jul 15, 2010 12:49 am Well, I think there's a difference when "inability to connect with people" is a main theme, and what's shown onscreen when people interact doesn't seem to correspond with what we're supposed to believe is happening all the rest of the time that we're not looking at them.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Jul 15, 2010 1:08 am I never saw anything on screen that made me think "Yeah, these two characters are deeply connecting". When I watch Before Sunrise I think "these two characters are deeply connecting", when I watch the characters in NGE I think "yeah, they're superficially getting along fine", which really has nothing to do with deeply connecting. Plus, I also think there's a difference between genuine connection and the perception of that connection. Shinji doesn't feel anyone really loves or cares for him because he doesn't love and care for himself, but we know this to be absolutely false when both Misato and Rei sacrifice themselves for him. I think that's one of NGE's many paradoxes in that we can't help but connect with each other by the mere act of being around each other, but the level of that connection and how we perceive that connection can be completely different.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Jul 15, 2010 1:54 am esselfortium wrote: The writing seems to intentionally work its way around some things that would have a decent chance of coming up in conversation. Like "So Rei, what are your parents like?"

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Thu Jul 15, 2010 2:03 am Mr. Tines wrote: I think you're meant to take the daily-life/banter scenes as being representative of the "nothing much happens" time, when the kids are in school, doing homework or yet another of the synch tests. You know, exactly like in any other fiction.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Jul 15, 2010 2:14 am If you don't know what it is to be all alone in a crowd, I'm not sure I can explain it.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Thu Jul 15, 2010 7:40 am It doesn't need any explanation when you consider the charge could be leveled against almost any work of fiction.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Jul 15, 2010 10:07 am esselfortium wrote: "So Rei, what are your parents like?"

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Thu Jul 15, 2010 1:19 pm Not that any of them would talk to her anyways. She isolates herself really well. Only Asuka, Hikari, or the stooges would ever get close enough to consider asking, and, well... Asuka wouldn't waste her time, Hikari is more attached to Asuka, Toji and Kensuke aren't the types to ask, and Shinji has all the answer he needs (the state of her apartment).

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Thu Jul 15, 2010 1:32 pm How about "Hey First! We should be friends! It'd be conveni-- Holy shit! Your hair is blue! " And then the entire schoolyard erupts with murmuring as every student simultaneously notices Rei's bizarre, unnatural appearance for the first time.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Thu Jul 15, 2010 1:51 pm Or not. Blue hair isn't that big of a deal, even if you ignore animu physics. Hair dye does exist after all.

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Thu Jul 15, 2010 1:52 pm And it's not like we saw the day she first came to class. They noticed the hair then, someone said something nice, she ignored them... And so it began. People got used to the unusual color, life went on. *gasp*

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Thu Jul 15, 2010 1:54 pm Legendary wrote: And it's not like we saw the day she first came to class. They noticed the hair then, someone said something nice, she ignored them... And so it began. People got used to the unusual color, life went on. *gasp*

Yeah, these two characters are deeply connecting". When I watch Before Sunrise I think "these two characters are deeply connecting", when I watch the characters in NGE I think "yeah, they're superficially getting along fine", which really has nothing to do with deeply connecting. Plus, I also think there's a difference between genuine connection and the perception of that connection. Shinji doesn't feel anyone really loves or cares for him because he doesn't love and care for himself, but we know this to be absolutely false when both Misato and Rei sacrifice themselves for him. I think that's one of NGE's many paradoxes in that we can't help but connect with each other by the mere act of being around each other, but the level of that connection and how we perceive that connection can be completely different.
